nativeRange.habitat: Coastal rocky reefs and coral reefs at depths of approximately 5–15 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on sponges, tunicates, and algae in the wild. In captivity, tends to accept mysis shrimp, frozen marine foods, krill, chopped clams, spirulina-based flake foods, and sponge-fortified angelfish preparations. Sponge content in the diet is generally considered important for long-term health.

goodWith: peaceful community fish in FOWLR systems

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: Totti. 3192x2394. Tack-sharp, vibrant full-body adult in reef setting showing diagnostic electric-blue horizontal stripes on mustard body, dark white-edged fins and yellow caudal fin.
Photographer: Derek Ramsey (Ram-Man). 1883x1057. Crisp clean side profile of an adult against soft background; clearly shows blue horizontal stripes on mustard body and yellow tail.
Photographer: Phuong Huy. 3120x3505. In-situ shot of adult among live rock; correct ID and colour but partly obscured and shares frame with other fish, so a lower-ranked variety extra.

summary: The Blueline Angelfish (Chaetodontoplus septentrionalis) is a striking Western Pacific species considered advanced in care, best suited to large, well-established FOWLR aquariums of 700 liters or more.
careNotes:
The Blueline Angelfish is generally considered a more challenging species, requiring a large, well-established aquarium with ample live rock for grazing and hiding. Juveniles display bold blue and white vertical stripes; adults develop electric blue horizontal stripes on a dark mustard body — this dramatic color change means juveniles may not be immediately recognizable as the adult form.

This species tends to be shy when first introduced and may take time to accept prepared foods. Offering sponge-based angelfish preparations, along with a variety of frozen meaty foods and marine algae, has been reported to improve acclimation and long-term health.

The Blueline Angelfish has been known to nip at stony and soft corals, clam mantles, and sessile invertebrates, and is generally not considered reef-safe.

Conspecific and heterospecific aggression toward other angelfish is well-documented, and this species is best kept singly unless a mated pair is established. Keeping multiple large angelfish together is generally advised against in aquariums under around 680 liters.

Like most members of the family Pomacanthidae, the Blueline Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning all individuals begin life as females, and the dominant fish in a group will eventually change sex to become male. Captive breeding is rarely reported.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has been described as rare and challenging. The species is oviparous with external fertilization, consistent with other Pomacanthidae. No established captive-breeding protocol is widely documented in the hobby literature.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (25 cm), tank minimum (500 L / 130 gal), care level (moderate to difficult), temperature (24–28°C), salinity (1.020–1.025), reef-safe (no), temperament (semi-aggressive), and oviparous breeding notes.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely reported. The species reproduces by releasing eggs and sperm into the water column for external fertilization, consistent with other members of Pomacanthidae. No established captive-breeding protocol is widely documented in the hobby.
